_meta.case: Trommel v. Attorney General of Canada
_meta.location: Langley, BC — Langley RCMP, Brookswood detachment
_meta.note: This is the canonical data source. Edit here, then sync to ios/Sources/Models/CaseData.swift (macOS symlinks it) and nulljosh.github.io/brief/script.js.

facts.value: Pre-existing fracture aggravated by prone restraint — additional physical harm head

grounds.title: Excessive Force
grounds.description: Officers knelt on subject's back while prone, causing respiratory distress consistent with positional asphyxia — a documented cause of in-custody death. No crime committed. Father witnessed. Pre-existing wrist fracture directly aggravated by prone restraint — elevates special damages and supports an independent physical harm head.
grounds.citation: Elmardy v. TPSB, 2019 ONSC 2931 ($130k) · Degen v. Min. Public Safety, 2023 BCSC ($317k — Surrey RCMP, PTSD, positional force)

grounds.title: Punitive Conduct
grounds.description: Sustained 7-step cascade of misconduct: unlawful entry, physical assault, arrest without grounds, forced medication, solitary confinement, overnight hold, discharge with no aftercare. Each step a choice. Officers responded to a psychiatric wellness call with zero psychiatric support.

grounds.title: PTSD General
grounds.description: Every day affected since August 1, 2023. No good days. Formal PTSD assessment underway May 2026. Causation to incident to be confirmed by treating therapist. Separate from future earning capacity head.
grounds.citation: Non-pecuniary damages — pain, suffering, loss of dignity

grounds.title: Forced Medication
grounds.description: Involuntary antipsychotics administered without consent. s.7 Charter breach (security of the person) + battery tort. Crown defense under MHA s.31 'deemed consent' is under active constitutional challenge (CCD v. AG BC, BCSC trial May 2025, decision pending).
grounds.citation: Fleming v. Ontario [2019] SCC 45 · CCD v. AG BC (BCSC 2025, pending)

grounds.title: Unlawful Entry
grounds.description: No Feeney warrant. No genuine exigent circumstances — subject answered door, spoke coherently, exercised right to walk away. Highest-tier s.8 breach. Root violation that enabled the chain of events.

grounds.title: Arbitrary Detention
grounds.description: Walking away during a non-arrest wellness call is not flight — it is a legal right. Detention began at physical restraint with no lawful authority. Overnight hold with no charge.

grounds.title: Solitary Confinement
grounds.description: Overnight solitary confinement with no charge. Subject in mental health crisis — isolation is the clinical opposite of appropriate care. Released with no aftercare plan, no referral, no follow-up.

grounds.title: Denial of Counsel
grounds.description: Upon detention, s.10(b) requires immediate notification of right to counsel and a reasonable opportunity to exercise it. Detention established at moment of physical restraint. Crown must demonstrate compliance — no compliance = automatic breach.
grounds.citation: R. v. Bartle [1994] 3 SCR 173 · R. v. Sinclair [2010] 2 SCR 310

checklist.label: Paul Kent declined May 18 — contact Thomas Harding & Neil Chantler (PK referrals — Degen case)
PTSD assessment started (therapy) — get Dx letter
Body cam footage requested from RCMP
Police report — both Daryls full names
CRCC complaint filed (RCMP is federal — OPCC is BC municipal only)
Therapist letter: PTSD Dx + period of incapacity re: this claim + causation to Aug 1, 2023
Pin discovery date: when you first understood a civil Charter claim was an appropriate remedy
ATIP filed with RCMP — officer names, notebooks (Form 1624), BWC footage, File #2023-25586
FOI filed with E-Comm 9-1-1 BC — 911 audio + CAD notes (neutralizes Godoy scope argument)
Email outreach sent to: Cameron Ward (declined — no longer practising), Arvay Finlay (declined May 25 — Robin Gage, no capacity), Klein Lawyers, BCCLA — email, don't call
Contact Thomas Harding (referred by Paul Kent — did Degen case $317k)
Contact Neil Chantler (referred by Paul Kent)

limitation.argument_1: Discoverability s.8(1)(d) — clock runs from when plaintiff knew a civil proceeding was appropriate, not from the harm. Discovery date: May 11, 2026.
limitation.argument_2: Mental incapacity s.18 — PTSD constituted domain-specific incapacity re: this claim. Therapy began May 10, 2026.
limitation.crown_dilemma: Crown cannot simultaneously argue PTSD was not severe enough for s.18 (to defeat limitation) and argue no permanent injury (to limit damages). Same PTSD. Same severity standard.

journal_seed.text: Discovery date established. Formally confirmed RCMP file #2023-25586 (August 1, 2023), began active legal research, retained a lawyer for consultation, and first understood that a civil proceeding under Ward v. Vancouver (City) [2010] 2 SCR 27 — a s.24(1) Charter damages claim — is an available and appropriate remedy. This constitutes discovery under BC Limitation Act s.8(1)(d). Called Paul Kent-Snowsell 3x; callback confirmed May 12. Contemporaneous record created.
Started PTSD therapy today. Formal assessment underway — building the clinical paper trail. First session directly connected current symptoms to the August 1, 2023 incident.
No good days since August 1, 2023. Every day is affected without exception.
Wakes with stomach aches and night sweats daily. Coping mechanisms developed in direct response to the trauma.
Nightmares with recurring themes. Worsened in intensity since the incident.
Incident replays on loop daily. Functioning prior to August 1, 2023. Significantly affected since.
Family relationships permanently damaged as a direct result of how police handled the incident.

scenarios.name: Most likely
scenarios.rationale: AG Canada settles to avoid precedent on forced psychiatric medication + solitary + Charter violations. No complicating factors.

scenarios.name: Trial
scenarios.rationale: Full trial victory with punitive damages and clear Charter breaches
